main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Shared-AE: an autoencoder framework with a novel regularization term to identify shared and private features between neural and behavioral data","Use of Cauchy-Schwarz (CS) divergence to extract shared information across modalities","Use of inverse CS divergence to capture private features unique to each modality","Application of Hidden Markov Model (HMM) to identify aligned motifs between neural and behavioral modalities"],"problems":["Automatic identification of shared subspaces/features between high-dimensional neural and behavioral activity","Separation of shared and private features in multi-modal neural and behavioral data","Reducing modality leakage in latent representations","Alignment of neural activity with complex, high-dimensional behaviors, including social interactions"],"datasets":["Head-fixed mouse performing a self-initiated decision-making task (2AFC dataset) with widefield calcium imaging (WFCI)","Freely-moving mouse engaged in social behavior with widefield calcium imaging (WFCI)"],"metrics":["Variance explained","Performance on paired and unpaired tasks (e.g., shuffled modality evaluation)","Alignment between neural and behavioral modalities","Robustness to missing or corrupted modality data"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance on 2AFC dataset (paired and unpaired tasks)","value":"Shared-AE outperforms existing models such as Gondur et al. (2024), Sani et al. (2021; 2024), Shi et al. (2019)"},{"metric":"Variance explained","value":"Improved variance explained compared to previous methods (exact values not specified in provided text)"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of a novel regularization term in an autoencoder to explicitly separate shared and private latent spaces, improving interpretability and reducing information leakage between modalities","Robustness to missing or corrupted modality data, enabling inference even when one modality is unavailable during testing","Superior performance on both paired and unpaired tasks, maintaining integrity of latent representations even when modalities are shuffled","Ability to handle complex data types such as raw image data and extend to more than three modalities, unlike previous methods","Minimization of distribution distance between learned representations instead of fitting to predefined priors, yielding more flexible and meaningful latent spaces","Improved utility for downstream tasks and enhanced variance explained due to explicit separation of shared and private features"]}

methods: Shared-AE: an autoencoder framework with a novel regularization term to identify shared and private features between neural and behavioral data
Use of Cauchy-Schwarz (CS) divergence to extract shared information across modalities
Use of inverse CS divergence to capture private features unique to each modality
Application of Hidden Markov Model (HMM) to identify aligned motifs between neural and behavioral modalities
problems: Automatic identification of shared subspaces/features between high-dimensional neural and behavioral activity
Separation of shared and private features in multi-modal neural and behavioral data
Reducing modality leakage in latent representations
Alignment of neural activity with complex, high-dimensional behaviors, including social interactions
datasets: Head-fixed mouse performing a self-initiated decision-making task (2AFC dataset) with widefield calcium imaging (WFCI)
Freely-moving mouse engaged in social behavior with widefield calcium imaging (WFCI)
metrics: Performance on paired and unpaired tasks (e.g., shuffled modality evaluation)
Alignment between neural and behavioral modalities
Robustness to missing or corrupted modality data

results.metric: Performance on 2AFC dataset (paired and unpaired tasks)
results.value: Shared-AE outperforms existing models such as Gondur et al. (2024), Sani et al. (2021; 2024), Shi et al. (2019)
Improved variance explained compared to previous methods (exact values not specified in provided text)

novelty_claims: Introduction of a novel regularization term in an autoencoder to explicitly separate shared and private latent spaces, improving interpretability and reducing information leakage between modalities
Robustness to missing or corrupted modality data, enabling inference even when one modality is unavailable during testing
Superior performance on both paired and unpaired tasks, maintaining integrity of latent representations even when modalities are shuffled
Ability to handle complex data types such as raw image data and extend to more than three modalities, unlike previous methods
Minimization of distribution distance between learned representations instead of fitting to predefined priors, yielding more flexible and meaningful latent spaces
Improved utility for downstream tasks and enhanced variance explained due to explicit separation of shared and private features

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["CEBRA: a new encoding method that jointly uses behavioural and neural data in a supervised or self-supervised manner to produce latent embeddings"],"problems":["Lack of nonlinear techniques to explicitly and flexibly leverage joint behavioural and neural data to uncover neural dynamics","Need for consistent and high-performance latent spaces for mapping behavioural actions to neural activity","Difficulty in producing consistent latent spaces across different recording modalities and sessions"],"datasets":["Calcium imaging datasets","Electrophysiology datasets (including Neuropixels)","Datasets from sensory and motor tasks across species","Natural video datasets for visual cortex decoding"],"metrics":["Consistency","Decoding accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Decoding accuracy (natural videos from visual cortex)","value":"Rapid, high-accuracy decoding demonstrated"},{"metric":"Consistency of latent spaces","value":"Consistent latent spaces produced across two-photon and Neuropixels data"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of CEBRA, a method that jointly uses behavioural and neural data in both supervised and self-supervised settings to produce latent embeddings","Ability to produce consistent latent spaces across different recording modalities and sessions","Flexible use for both hypothesis-driven and label-free discovery-driven analysis","Application to both single- and multi-session datasets, and across species and behaviours","Demonstration of rapid, high-accuracy decoding of natural videos from visual cortex"]}

methods: CEBRA: a new encoding method that jointly uses behavioural and neural data in a supervised or self-supervised manner to produce latent embeddings
problems: Lack of nonlinear techniques to explicitly and flexibly leverage joint behavioural and neural data to uncover neural dynamics
Need for consistent and high-performance latent spaces for mapping behavioural actions to neural activity
Difficulty in producing consistent latent spaces across different recording modalities and sessions
datasets: Calcium imaging datasets
Electrophysiology datasets (including Neuropixels)
Datasets from sensory and motor tasks across species
Natural video datasets for visual cortex decoding

results.metric: Decoding accuracy (natural videos from visual cortex)
results.value: Rapid, high-accuracy decoding demonstrated
Consistent latent spaces produced across two-photon and Neuropixels data

novelty_claims: Introduction of CEBRA, a method that jointly uses behavioural and neural data in both supervised and self-supervised settings to produce latent embeddings
Ability to produce consistent latent spaces across different recording modalities and sessions
Flexible use for both hypothesis-driven and label-free discovery-driven analysis
Application to both single- and multi-session datasets, and across species and behaviours
Demonstration of rapid, high-accuracy decoding of natural videos from visual cortex

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Multi-modal Gaussian Process Variational Autoencoder (MM-GPVAE)","Parameterization of latent space in the Fourier domain","Partitioning of latent variables into shared and independent components across modalities","Linear mapping of latents to neural data (as in GPFA)","Nonlinear mapping of latents to other modalities via deep neural networks (as in GP-VAE)"],"problems":["Joint modeling of neural and behavioral (multi-modal) time-series data","Extracting shared and independent latent structure across different experimental data modalities","Improving interpretability and flexibility of latent variable models for multi-modal neuroscience data","Overcoming limitations of existing models that use a single latent space or lack temporal structure"],"datasets":["Simulated multi-modal data: Poisson spike counts and MNIST images (rotating and scaling)","Drosophila whole-brain calcium imaging with tracked limb positions","Manduca sexta (hawkmoth) spike train measurements from ten wing muscles with visual stimulus"],"metrics":["Latent identification accuracy","Reconstruction accuracy of images and neural rates on held-out trials","Separation of behavioral conditions in latent space"],"results":[{"metric":"Latent identification accuracy (simulated data)","value":"Improved over standard GP-VAE methods (quantitative value not specified)"},{"metric":"Reconstruction accuracy (images and neural rates, simulated data)","value":"Good reconstructions on held-out trials (quantitative value not specified)"},{"metric":"Separation of behavioral conditions (Drosophila data)","value":"Neural and shared subspaces best separate behavioral conditions (qualitative result)"},{"metric":"Tracking of time-varying latent components (Manduca sexta data)","value":"Distinct latent components track muscle and stimulus movement (qualitative result)"}],"novelty_claims":["First unsupervised latent variable model to extract temporally evolving shared and independent latents for distinct, simultaneously recorded experimental modalities in neuroscience.","Parameterization of latent time-series in the Fourier domain for improved latent identification in GP-VAE models.","Flexible model that partitions latent variability into shared and independent components, with both linear and nonlinear mappings to different modalities.","Demonstration of the model's adaptability to diverse real-world multi-modal neuroscience datasets."]}

methods: Multi-modal Gaussian Process Variational Autoencoder (MM-GPVAE)
Parameterization of latent space in the Fourier domain
Partitioning of latent variables into shared and independent components across modalities
Linear mapping of latents to neural data (as in GPFA)
Nonlinear mapping of latents to other modalities via deep neural networks (as in GP-VAE)
problems: Joint modeling of neural and behavioral (multi-modal) time-series data
Extracting shared and independent latent structure across different experimental data modalities
Improving interpretability and flexibility of latent variable models for multi-modal neuroscience data
Overcoming limitations of existing models that use a single latent space or lack temporal structure
datasets: Simulated multi-modal data: Poisson spike counts and MNIST images (rotating and scaling)
Drosophila whole-brain calcium imaging with tracked limb positions
Manduca sexta (hawkmoth) spike train measurements from ten wing muscles with visual stimulus
metrics: Reconstruction accuracy of images and neural rates on held-out trials
Separation of behavioral conditions in latent space

results.value: Improved over standard GP-VAE methods (quantitative value not specified)

results.metric: Reconstruction accuracy (images and neural rates, simulated data)
results.value: Good reconstructions on held-out trials (quantitative value not specified)
Neural and shared subspaces best separate behavioral conditions (qualitative result)

results.metric: Tracking of time-varying latent components (Manduca sexta data)
results.value: Distinct latent components track muscle and stimulus movement (qualitative result)

novelty_claims: First unsupervised latent variable model to extract temporally evolving shared and independent latents for distinct, simultaneously recorded experimental modalities in neuroscience.
Parameterization of latent time-series in the Fourier domain for improved latent identification in GP-VAE models.
Flexible model that partitions latent variability into shared and independent components, with both linear and nonlinear mappings to different modalities.
Demonstration of the model's adaptability to diverse real-world multi-modal neuroscience datasets.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Constrained Subspace Variational Autoencoder (CS-VAE)","Semi-supervised partitioning of behavioral subspace","Cauchy-Schwarz Divergence (CSD) regularization for latent separation"],"problems":["Disentangling behavioral features that are common across subjects and those that are subject-specific in multi-subject behavioral data","Modeling and analyzing social interactions and individual behaviors in freely-moving, naturalistic settings","Lack of tools for efficient and interpretable analysis of high-dimensional behavioral video data across subjects","Difficulty in separating individual and social components in behavioral data without labeled subject/session information"],"datasets":["Multi-subject behavioral video datasets (e.g., mice performing tasks)","Social interaction behavioral video datasets (e.g., two animals in a freely moving task)"],"metrics":["Ability to recover behavioral motifs across animals","Efficiency of decoding novel subject’s behavior","Quality of latent variable disentanglement (qualitative and quantitative)"],"results":[{"metric":"Behavioral motif recovery","value":"Successfully recovered same motifs across different animals and social interaction motifs in freely moving tasks"},{"metric":"Decoding efficiency","value":"Demonstrated efficient decoding of novel subject’s behavior in a unified model"},{"metric":"Latent disentanglement","value":"CS-VAE achieved separation of individual and social/background features in latent space"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of the constrained subspace variational autoencoder (CS-VAE) for disentangling multi-subject and social behavioral representations","Novel use of Cauchy-Schwarz Divergence (CSD) as a variational regularizer to separate latent variables corresponding to individual and social/background features","First approach to flexibly partition behavioral latent space in naturalistic, multi-subject, and social settings without requiring labeled subject/session information","Facilitates downstream analysis such as motif recovery and efficient decoding of novel subjects in behavioral neuroscience"]}

methods: Constrained Subspace Variational Autoencoder (CS-VAE)
Semi-supervised partitioning of behavioral subspace
Cauchy-Schwarz Divergence (CSD) regularization for latent separation
problems: Disentangling behavioral features that are common across subjects and those that are subject-specific in multi-subject behavioral data
Modeling and analyzing social interactions and individual behaviors in freely-moving, naturalistic settings
Lack of tools for efficient and interpretable analysis of high-dimensional behavioral video data across subjects
Difficulty in separating individual and social components in behavioral data without labeled subject/session information
datasets: Multi-subject behavioral video datasets (e.g., mice performing tasks)
Social interaction behavioral video datasets (e.g., two animals in a freely moving task)
metrics: Ability to recover behavioral motifs across animals
Efficiency of decoding novel subject’s behavior
Quality of latent variable disentanglement (qualitative and quantitative)

results.value: Successfully recovered same motifs across different animals and social interaction motifs in freely moving tasks
Demonstrated efficient decoding of novel subject’s behavior in a unified model
CS-VAE achieved separation of individual and social/background features in latent space

novelty_claims: Introduction of the constrained subspace variational autoencoder (CS-VAE) for disentangling multi-subject and social behavioral representations
Novel use of Cauchy-Schwarz Divergence (CSD) as a variational regularizer to separate latent variables corresponding to individual and social/background features
First approach to flexibly partition behavioral latent space in naturalistic, multi-subject, and social settings without requiring labeled subject/session information
Facilitates downstream analysis such as motif recovery and efficient decoding of novel subjects in behavioral neuroscience

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["pi-VAE (probabilistic identifiable Variational Autoencoder): a latent variable model that integrates task variables into the VAE framework for neural data analysis","Adaptation and generalization of identifiable VAE (iVAE) for neuroscience applications"],"problems":["Difficulty in interpreting and identifying latent variables in deep generative models (e.g., VAEs) applied to high-dimensional neural activity data","Need for statistical techniques that are both expressive and interpretable for analyzing large-scale neural recordings","Combining the strengths of regression-based and latent-based models for neural data analysis"],"datasets":["Synthetic neural data","Rat hippocampus population recordings during navigation task","Macaque motor cortex population recordings during reaching task"],"metrics":["Model fit (e.g., likelihood or reconstruction error)","Interpretability of latent variables","Identifiability of latent variables"],"results":[{"metric":"Model fit (qualitative)","value":"pi-VAE fits the data better than previous methods"},{"metric":"Interpretability/Identifiability (qualitative)","value":"pi-VAE provides more interpretable and identifiable latent variables, yielding novel insights into neural codes"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes pi-VAE, a new method that integrates task variables into the VAE framework to improve identifiability and interpretability of latent models for neural data","Generalizes and adapts identifiable VAE (iVAE) for neuroscience applications, making it suitable for a broad variety of datasets","Demonstrates that incorporating task variables into the latent model leads to more constrained, interpretable, and identifiable models of neural activity"]}

methods: pi-VAE (probabilistic identifiable Variational Autoencoder): a latent variable model that integrates task variables into the VAE framework for neural data analysis
Adaptation and generalization of identifiable VAE (iVAE) for neuroscience applications
problems: Difficulty in interpreting and identifying latent variables in deep generative models (e.g., VAEs) applied to high-dimensional neural activity data
Need for statistical techniques that are both expressive and interpretable for analyzing large-scale neural recordings
Combining the strengths of regression-based and latent-based models for neural data analysis
datasets: Synthetic neural data
Rat hippocampus population recordings during navigation task
Macaque motor cortex population recordings during reaching task
metrics: Model fit (e.g., likelihood or reconstruction error)

results.value: pi-VAE fits the data better than previous methods
pi-VAE provides more interpretable and identifiable latent variables, yielding novel insights into neural codes

novelty_claims: Proposes pi-VAE, a new method that integrates task variables into the VAE framework to improve identifiability and interpretability of latent models for neural data
Generalizes and adapts identifiable VAE (iVAE) for neuroscience applications, making it suitable for a broad variety of datasets
Demonstrates that incorporating task variables into the latent model leads to more constrained, interpretable, and identifiable models of neural activity

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Latent Factor Analysis via Dynamical Systems (LFADS)","Sequential auto-encoders for neural data analysis"],"problems":["Inferring latent neural population dynamics from single-trial neural spiking data","Overcoming challenges of incomplete sampling, trial-to-trial variability, and spike timing fluctuations in single-trial analysis","Combining data from non-overlapping recording sessions to improve inference of neural dynamics"],"datasets":["Macaque motor cortical datasets","Human motor cortical datasets"],"metrics":["Prediction accuracy of behavioral variables","Precision of firing rate estimates","Correlation between inferred perturbations and behavioral choices"],"results":[{"metric":"Prediction accuracy of behavioral variables","value":"LFADS accurately predicts observed behavioral variables (quantitative values not specified)"},{"metric":"Precision of firing rate estimates","value":"LFADS extracts precise firing rate estimates on single trials (quantitative values not specified)"},{"metric":"Correlation with behavioral choices","value":"LFADS infers perturbations to dynamics that correlate with behavioral choices (quantitative values not specified)"}],"novelty_claims":["LFADS is a deep learning method capable of inferring latent neural dynamics from single-trial spiking data, not just trial-averaged data.","LFADS can combine data from non-overlapping recording sessions spanning months to improve inference of underlying dynamics.","LFADS can infer perturbations to neural dynamics that correlate with behavioral choices.","LFADS enables extraction of precise firing rate estimates and neural dynamics at the single-trial level."]}

methods: Latent Factor Analysis via Dynamical Systems (LFADS)
Sequential auto-encoders for neural data analysis
problems: Inferring latent neural population dynamics from single-trial neural spiking data
Overcoming challenges of incomplete sampling, trial-to-trial variability, and spike timing fluctuations in single-trial analysis
Combining data from non-overlapping recording sessions to improve inference of neural dynamics

Human motor cortical datasets

novelty_claims: LFADS is a deep learning method capable of inferring latent neural dynamics from single-trial spiking data, not just trial-averaged data.
LFADS can combine data from non-overlapping recording sessions spanning months to improve inference of underlying dynamics.
LFADS can infer perturbations to neural dynamics that correlate with behavioral choices.
LFADS enables extraction of precise firing rate estimates and neural dynamics at the single-trial level.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Multimodal Variational Autoencoder (MVAE) for joint latent representation","Generation of a sparse teacher signal from MVAE to guide training of a reconstruction model","Integration of recent Dynamical Systems Reconstruction (DSR) training techniques (e.g., sparse/generalized teacher forcing)","Special loss terms and shared decoder models to ensure consistency between MVAE and reconstruction model latent codes"],"problems":["Reconstruction of nonlinear dynamical systems from time series data with multiple modalities (continuous, discrete, symbolic)","Integration of multimodal data (e.g., behavioral labels, spike counts, physiological recordings) for dynamical systems modeling","Enabling generative modeling and reconstruction of system dynamics from symbolic/categorical data alone","Overcoming limitations of existing DSR methods that assume single-modality, continuous, Gaussian-noise data"],"datasets":[],"metrics":[],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["First efficient and flexible framework for multimodal data integration specifically for dynamical systems reconstruction (DSR)","Novel use of a multimodal VAE to generate a sparse teacher signal for guiding DSR model training","Framework enables DS reconstruction from categorical/symbolic data alone, which was not possible with previous methods","Fully generative approach that produces trajectories with the same geometrical and temporal structure as the ground truth system","Special loss terms and shared decoder models ensure consistency between multimodal latent codes and reconstruction model, improving integration and reconstruction quality"]}

methods: Multimodal Variational Autoencoder (MVAE) for joint latent representation
Generation of a sparse teacher signal from MVAE to guide training of a reconstruction model
Integration of recent Dynamical Systems Reconstruction (DSR) training techniques (e.g., sparse/generalized teacher forcing)
Special loss terms and shared decoder models to ensure consistency between MVAE and reconstruction model latent codes
problems: Reconstruction of nonlinear dynamical systems from time series data with multiple modalities (continuous, discrete, symbolic)
Integration of multimodal data (e.g., behavioral labels, spike counts, physiological recordings) for dynamical systems modeling
Enabling generative modeling and reconstruction of system dynamics from symbolic/categorical data alone
Overcoming limitations of existing DSR methods that assume single-modality, continuous, Gaussian-noise data
novelty_claims: First efficient and flexible framework for multimodal data integration specifically for dynamical systems reconstruction (DSR)
Novel use of a multimodal VAE to generate a sparse teacher signal for guiding DSR model training
Framework enables DS reconstruction from categorical/symbolic data alone, which was not possible with previous methods
Fully generative approach that produces trajectories with the same geometrical and temporal structure as the ground truth system
Special loss terms and shared decoder models ensure consistency between multimodal latent codes and reconstruction model, improving integration and reconstruction quality

methods: Effective Number of Shared Dimensions (ENSD): a model-free measure for shared dimensionality between datasets
Decomposition of ENSD to describe geometric features of shared subspaces
Comparison of ENSD with Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) and Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA)
Application of ENSD to multi-modal analyses of multivariate datasets
problems: Lack of a standard, model-free measure for the dimensionality of shared subspaces between datasets
Difficulty in characterizing shared variability and structure between paired datasets, especially in high-dimensional, sparse, or low-rank data
Limitations of model-based approaches (e.g., need for sequential hypothesis testing, lack of generalizability)
datasets: Synthetic data (for method validation)
Convolutional neural network (CNN) layer activations
Neuroscience datasets: communication subspaces between brain regions
Sparse connectomic data from the fruit fly olfactory system

results.metric: ENSD vs. CKA and CCA on sparse data
results.value: ENSD is more robust and effective for sparse or low-rank data than CKA and CCA

results.metric: Application to fly olfactory connectomic data
results.value: ENSD reveals novel results about the interaction between innate and learned olfactory representations
ENSD is computationally efficient compared to model-based approaches

novelty_claims: Introduction of the Effective Number of Shared Dimensions (ENSD), a simple, interpretable, and model-free measure of shared dimensionality between datasets
ENSD generalizes the participation ratio to pairs of datasets and can be decomposed to describe geometric features of shared subspaces
ENSD is robust to sparse and low-rank data, unlike existing methods such as CCA
ENSD can be written as a similarity metric related to CKA but provides additional information about the dimensionality of aligned subspaces
Demonstration of ENSD's utility across synthetic, artificial neural network, and biological neural data, including novel insights into the fly olfactory system
